title: feat(mcp): add Redis MCP Bridge with 25 tools (#2511)
body:
## Summary

Implements **redis_mcp** as the 11th native MCP bridge in AutoBot's registry, giving AI agents direct access to Redis data structures, RediSearch vector search, and operational intelligence.

Closes #2511

### 25 MCP Tools across 3 categories:

**Data Access (15 tools):**
- String: `redis_get`, `redis_set`, `redis_delete`
- Hash: `redis_hget`, `redis_hgetall`, `redis_hset`
- List: `redis_lrange`, `redis_lpush`, `redis_rpush`
- Sorted Set: `redis_zrange`
- Stream: `redis_xrange`, `redis_xadd`
- Keys: `redis_scan_keys`, `redis_type`, `redis_ttl`

**Vector Search (4 tools):**
- `redis_vector_create_index`, `redis_vector_search`, `redis_hybrid_search`, `redis_vector_index_info`

**Ops Intelligence (6 tools):**
- `redis_server_info`, `redis_dbsize`, `redis_memory_stats`, `redis_stream_health`, `redis_client_list`, `redis_slowlog`

### RBAC Model:
- **Users:** Read all + write `autobot:agent:*` namespace only, read-only ops (no client_list/slowlog)
- **Admins:** Full access, destructive ops (delete) require approval

### Security hardening (code review findings):
- All keys validated in namespace check (not just the first) — prevents multi-key bypass
- `filter_expression` sanitized via regex allowlist — prevents RediSearch injection
- `top_k` clamped to [1, 500] — prevents resource exhaustion
- `create_index` restricted to SCOPED_WRITE for non-admin users
- `database` parameter validated against DATABASE_MAPPING

### Architecture:
- All tools use `autobot_shared.redis_client` (async, pooled, circuit-broken)
- Registered in both MCP registry (`mcp_registry.py`) and router registry (`core_routers.py`)
- Follows existing MCP bridge patterns (filesystem_mcp, database_mcp)
- Agent system prompts auto-discover Redis tools via `_get_mcp_tools_prompt()` (#2629)

### Files:
- `autobot-backend/api/redis_mcp/__init__.py` — Package entry
- `autobot-backend/api/redis_mcp/tools.py` — 25 tool definitions (JSON schemas)
- `autobot-backend/api/redis_mcp/rbac.py` — Role-based access control matrix
- `autobot-backend/api/redis_mcp/data_access.py` — 15 data tool handlers
- `autobot-backend/api/redis_mcp/vector_search.py` — 4 vector search handlers
- `autobot-backend/api/redis_mcp/ops_intelligence.py` — 6 ops tool handlers
- `autobot-backend/api/redis_mcp/router.py` — FastAPI router + dispatch

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify all 25 tools listed at `GET /api/redis/mcp/tools`
- [ ] Verify RBAC: user cannot access `redis_client_list` or `redis_slowlog`
- [ ] Verify namespace enforcement: user write to non-`autobot:agent:*` key is rejected
- [ ] Verify multi-key namespace enforcement: all keys in a delete call are validated
- [ ] Verify admin destructive ops return `approval_required` status
- [ ] Verify data access tools (get/set/hash/list/stream) work against Redis
- [ ] Verify vector search creates index and queries return results
- [ ] Verify `top_k` is clamped (passing 9999 returns max 500)
- [ ] Verify invalid `filter_expression` is rejected
- [ ] Verify invalid `database` parameter returns 400
- [ ] Verify ops tools return valid server stats
- [ ] Verify bridge appears in MCP registry at `GET /api/mcp/bridges`
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks pass (black, isort, flake8, bandit, function length)

title: fix: repair TCP port-remapping slow path
body:
## Summary

Fixes #2. Four bugs in the TCP port-remapping slow path. The slow path (triggered when the virtual port is already taken) had not been tested (all existing tests ran sequentially, so the port was always free by the time the second sandbox started).

## Bugs fixed

**Bug 1 - `_allocate_real_port` held socket blocked the child's bind**

The parent kept the allocated real port socket open to prevent stealing, then
rewrote the child's sockaddr to that port and sent CONTINUE. The child's `bind()` hit `EADDRINUSE` because the parent was already holding the same
address:port (only released later in `PortMap.close()`). 
Fixed by closing it immediately, accepting the same tiny race window already mentioned in `_try_reserve_port`.

**Bug 2 - `os.pidfd_open` not available in Python build**

`fixup_getsockname` used `os.pidfd_open` which raises `AttributeError` on some builds. 
Fixed by using `_libc.syscall(_NR_PIDFD_OPEN)` directly, consistent with the
rest of the codebase. In the future, it makes sense to separate all syscalls into
a different file like `_syscall.py`, since an identical `_NR_PIDFD_OPEN` was
found in `_context.py`.

**Bug 3 - `fixup_getsockname` leaked the duplicated fd**

`s.detach()` does not call `os.close()`, so `local_fd` was never closed on the
success path. The leaked fd kept the child's socket alive after the child
exited, holding the port bound in the parent. Fixed by moving\ `os.close(local_fd)` into the `finally` block.

**Bug 4 - `NotifSupervisor.stop()` never called `port_map.close()`**

Proxy listener sockets were left running after sandbox teardown. Fixed by
calling `port_map.close()` at the end of `stop()`.

## Tests

Two new tests added to `TestPortRemap`:

- `test_slow_path_host_holds_virtual_port`: parent process holds the virtual
  port, forcing the slow path without threads or timing dependencies.
- `test_slow_path_two_concurrent_sandboxes`: two sandboxes run concurrently;  sandbox 1 holds the port open while sandbox 2 must remap.

title: Python 3 Migration and Architectural Refactor (v0.2.0)
body:
This PR completes the migration of PyStormTracker to modern Python 3 standards.

### Key Changes:
- **Python 3 & Type Hinting**: Fully migrated from Python 2; added comprehensive type hints verified by mypy and uff.
- **Library Migration**: Switched from deprecated PyNIO to 
etCDF4. Fixed issues with automatic coordinate scaling.
- **Architectural Refactor**: Organized code into models/ (data structures) and simple/ (core logic).
- **Modern Parallelization**: Added **Dask** as the default backend with automatic worker detection, alongside existing **MPI** support.
- **CLI Enhancements**: Modernized the CLI using rgparse and added human-readable CSV export (datetime strings, 4-decimal precision).
- **Testing**: Integrated 17 unit and integration tests verifying consistency across Serial, Dask, and MPI backends.
- **Documentation**: Updated README with professional history (NCAR SIParCS 2015), technical methodology, and formal academic citations (Yau and Chang, 2020).

title: Code quality improvements (Issues #4, #5, #10, #11)
body:
## Summary
Code quality and maintainability improvements addressing technical debt and modernization.

Fixes #4, #5, #10, #11

## Issues Addressed

### Issue #4: Remove Python 2 Compatibility ✅
- Removed Python 2 `raw_input` compatibility from `scan.py`
- Removed Python 2 `shlex` import fallback from `plex.py`
- Cleaned up legacy Python 2 code (Python 2 EOL: January 2020)
- **Impact**: Cleaner codebase, modern Python 3 only

### Issue #5: HTTP Request Timeouts ✅
- **Status**: Already implemented!
- Verified all `requests.get/post/put` calls in `plex.py` have `timeout=30`
- Verified Google Drive API calls have `timeout=30` 
- Verified rclone API calls have `timeout=120`
- **No changes needed** - this issue was already resolved

### Issue #10: Move HTML to Jinja2 Templates ✅
- Created `templates/` directory
- Moved inline HTML to 3 Jinja2 templates:
  - `manual_scan.html` - Manual scan form
  - `scan_success.html` - Success message
  - `scan_error.html` - Error message
- Updated `scan.py` to use `render_template()`
- **Benefits**: Automatic XSS protection, easier maintenance, template inheritance

### Issue #11: Database Connection Pooling ✅
- Implemented `PooledSqliteDatabase` from `playhouse.pool`
- Configured max_connections=8, stale_timeout=300
- Added SQLite optimizations:
  - WAL mode for better concurrency
  - 64MB cache size
  - Optimized synchronous mode
- **Benefits**: Better performance, reduced connection overhead, automatic connection management

## Files Changed
- **Modified**: `db.py`, `plex.py`, `scan.py`
- **New**: `templates/manual_scan.html`, `templates/scan_success.html`, `templates/scan_error.html`

## Technical Details

**Python 2 Removal**:
```python
# Removed from scan.py
if hasattr(__builtins__, 'raw_input'):
    input = raw_input

# Removed from plex.py
try:
    from shlex import quote as cmd_quote
except ImportError:
    from pipes import quote as cmd_quote
```

**Template Usage**:
```python
# Before (inline HTML)
return """<!DOCTYPE html>..."""

# After (Jinja2 template)
return render_template('scan_success.html', path=final_path)
```

**Connection Pooling**:
```python
# Before
database = SqliteDatabase(db_path, threadlocals=True)

# After
database = PooledSqliteDatabase(
    db_path,
    max_connections=8,
    stale_timeout=300,
    pragmas={'journal_mode': 'wal', 'cache_size': -1024 * 64}
)
```

## Security Improvements
- ✅ Jinja2 automatic HTML escaping prevents XSS
- ✅ Template separation improves code review
- ✅ Connection pooling prevents resource exhaustion

## Performance Improvements
- ✅ Connection pooling reduces DB overhead
- ✅ WAL mode improves concurrent access
- ✅ 64MB cache reduces disk I/O

## Backward Compatibility
✅ **All changes are backward compatible**
✅ No breaking changes to existing functionality
✅ Existing config files work unchanged

## Testing Recommendations
1. Test manual scan form renders correctly
2. Verify success/error templates display properly
3. Test database operations with connection pooling
4. Verify no Python 2 code remains
5. Performance test with concurrent scans

## Dependencies
Requires PR #14 (dependency updates) to be merged first.
✅ PR #14 has been merged

title: refactor: migrate from dataclasses to Pydantic models
body:
## Summary

This PR refactors the configuration models from Python dataclasses to Pydantic models, providing better validation, clearer error messages, and simplified configuration loading.

## Changes

### Models (`models.py`)
- Replaced all `@dataclass` decorators with Pydantic `BaseModel`
- Converted `__post_init__` validation to `@field_validator` and `@model_validator`
- Updated `field()` to `Field()` for default factories
- Added `model_config` to `ReplicationConfig` to handle schema field shadowing

### Config Manager (`manager.py`)
- Simplified `load_config()` to use deep merge + Pydantic validation
- Removed manual parsing of nested config structures (Pydantic handles this)
- Added `_save_defaults()` function to generate defaults.yaml from Pydantic models
- Added `main()` function to allow regenerating defaults.yaml via CLI
- Added `DefaultsReplicationConfig` to imports

### Tests
- Updated tests to expect `ValidationError` instead of `ValueError` for Pydantic validation
- Added `from pydantic import ValidationError` imports
- Updated error message patterns to match Pydantic's error format

### Dependencies
- Added `pydantic>=2.0.0` to pyproject.toml

## Benefits

1. **Better Validation**: Pydantic provides automatic type checking and validation with clear error messages
2. **Simplified Code**: Reduced manual parsing logic in `load_config()` from ~160 lines to ~80 lines
3. **Type Safety**: Better IDE support and type checking
4. **Defaults Management**: Can now regenerate defaults.yaml from code using `python -m mongo_replication.config.manager`
5. **Deep Merge**: Simplified deep merging of defaults with user configs

## Testing

All 270 unit tests pass:
- ✅ All config model tests
- ✅ All config transformation tests
- ✅ All other unit tests

Run tests with:
```bash
uv run pytest tests/unit/ -v
```

title: BN.4: Control-Chain Orchestrator — mandatory gate enforcement for decision-bearing stages
body:
BN.3 proved gating works but left it advisory — nothing forced it to run. This PR closes that gap by introducing a single canonical execution path that makes enforcement + gating non-optional for `recommend`, `synthesis`, and `export` stages. Also fixes two BN.3 bugs surfaced during implementation.

## New: `control_chain.py` — single mandatory entry point

```python
from spectrum_systems.modules.runtime.control_chain import run_control_chain

result = run_control_chain(raw_input, stage="synthesis")
# continuation_allowed=False if gating was skipped OR gating_outcome=="halt"
# No path exists to reach continuation_allowed=True without a completed gating step
```

Accepts evaluation, enforcement, or gating artifacts (auto-detected). Routes each through the appropriate chain:

| Input kind | Chain executed |
|---|---|
| `evaluation` | enforcement → gating → decision |
| `enforcement` | gating → decision |
| `gating` | validate + return (audit mode) |

Enforcement alone **cannot** allow continuation at decision-bearing stages — `check_mandatory_gating` hard-fails if `gating_executed=False` regardless of enforcement status.

## New: `slo_control_chain_decision` schema + artifact

`contracts/schemas/slo_control_chain_decision.schema.json` — all fields required, `additionalProperties: false`. Key fields: `continuation_allowed`, `blocking_layer` (`none|enforcement|gating|orchestration`), `primary_reason_code`, `stage_source` (`original|override`).

Reason codes: `control_chain_continue`, `control_chain_continue_with_warning`, `control_chain_blocked_by_gating`, `control_chain_blocked_by_missing_gating`, `control_chain_blocked_by_malformed_input`, `control_chain_blocked_by_inconsistent_state`.

## New: `scripts/run_slo_control_chain.py` CLI

Exit codes: `0` continue, `1` continue_with_warning, `2` blocked, `3` error. Halt (`2`) takes precedence — never conflated with `3` regardless of schema errors.

## BN.3 bug fixes

- **Exit code precedence** (`run_slo_gating.py`): `halt` previously returned `3` when schema errors were also present. Fixed — `halt` always returns `2`.
- **Fallback visibility** (`decision_gating.py`): silent fallback to built-in stage postures when `slo_gating_rules.json` is unavailable now emits a warning into the gating artifact and sets `_GATING_CONFIG_FALLBACK_USED`.

## Tests

113 new tests in `tests/test_slo_control_chain.py` covering all 24 requirement categories including: bypass prevention, fail-closed on crashes, schema validation, exit code non-overlap, stage/policy override, BN.1–BN.3 artifact compatibility, and determinism. Full suite: 2869 passed.

title: fix: restore transcript pipeline (cron + router)
body:
## Summary

- **Cron dead since March 20 11:05**: `repo-sync-cron.sh` sources `.env.system-paths` which uses `$USER`, but cron doesn't set it. With `set -euo pipefail`, the script dies on `unbound variable`. Fix: `export USER="${USER:-$(whoami)}"` before sourcing.
- **Hook-on-Stop never worked** (since March 11): `router.py` reads `ctx.raw_input.get("transcript_path")` but `normalize_input()` pops that field into `ctx.transcript_path`. The raw_input lookup always returns None. Fix: use `ctx.transcript_path`.

Both breaks meant no new transcripts, summaries, dashboard synthesis, or daily note data since March 20.

## Test plan

- [x] Verified cron fix by running `env -u USER bash scripts/repo-sync-cron.sh transcript` — successfully generated missed transcripts
- [ ] Verify hook fix by ending a session and checking for new transcript in `$AOPS_SESSIONS/transcripts/`

title: Fix for Empty except
body:
To fix the problem, we should (1) use correct Python 3 exception syntax and (2) avoid an empty `except` that only `pass`-es without explanation. The minimal behavior-preserving fix is to catch `(TypeError, ValueError)` and either log the error or, if logging is considered too noisy, add a clear comment explaining why errors are intentionally ignored. Since we cannot assume an existing logging setup, but PyQt applications commonly print or use `QMessageBox` selectively, the least invasive and still CodeQL-acceptable approach is to keep ignoring the error but add an explanatory comment and use the tuple exception syntax. That way, malformed breadth data from `QSettings` is skipped explicitly and the rest of the app continues unchanged.

Concretely, in `app/main_window.py` around lines 621–624, replace:

```py
                try:
                    self._col_widths[tab] = [int(w) for w in raw]
                except TypeError, ValueError:
                    pass
```

with:

```py
                try:
                    self._col_widths[tab] = [int(w) for w in raw]
                except (TypeError, ValueError):
                    # Ignore malformed or non-integer column width settings and fall back to defaults.
                    pass
```

This keeps all existing functionality (bad settings are ignored) while satisfying the static analysis rule and fixing the Python 3 exception syntax.


_Suggested fixes powered by Copilot Autofix. Review carefully before merging._

title: Fix CI #16: resolve pylint violations and Python 3.13 compatibility
body:
CI #16 failed due to pylint violations across 6 files (exit code 1, 9.46/10 score) and Python 3.13 version constraint mismatch. All issues are resolved without disabling pylint globally.

## What's Changed

### Circular import eliminated (`diffsync/models.py` ↔ `diffsync/adapters.py`)
- `_is_zabbix_target()` previously imported `ZabbixRemoteAdapter` at call time to do `isinstance` — causing `R0401` cyclic-import and `C0415` import-outside-toplevel
- Replaced with a marker attribute check: `getattr(adapter, "IS_ZABBIX_REMOTE", False)`
- Added `IS_ZABBIX_REMOTE = True` to `ZabbixRemoteAdapter`; compatible with `MagicMock(spec=ZabbixRemoteAdapter)` in tests

### Deferred imports moved to module top-level
| File | Symbol | Pylint code |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `diffsync/models.py` | `get_zabbix_client_from_config` | C0415 (×3) |
| `diffsync/adapters.py` | `nautobot.dcim.models.Device` | C0415 |
| `jobs.py` | `django.conf.settings`, `DataMapping` | C0415 (×4) |
| `utils/zabbix.py` | `django.conf.settings` | C0415 |

Tests that patched `utils.zabbix.get_zabbix_client_from_config` for model CRUD tests updated to patch `diffsync.models.get_zabbix_client_from_config` — the correct location after top-level import.

### Inline pylint disables for intentional patterns
- `broad-exception-caught` (W0718) on two adapter catch blocks — alongside existing `# noqa: BLE001`
- `too-few-public-methods` (R0903) on `Meta` inner classes in `jobs.py` and test class in `test_zabbix_diff.py`
- `too-many-arguments` / `too-many-locals` (R0913/R0914) on `ZabbixClient.build_interface` and `upsert_host`

### Test cleanup
- All local imports inside test methods moved to module top-level
- Empty-sequence assertion: `list(...) == []` → `not any(...)` (C1803)

### Python 3.13 support (`pyproject.toml` + `poetry.lock`)
- Updated `requires-python` from `>=3.10,<3.13` to `>=3.10,<3.14` to match CI matrix which tests Python 3.13
- Updated `[tool.poetry.dependencies] python` from `>=3.10,<3.13` to `>=3.10,<3.14`
- Added `Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13` classifier
- Regenerated `poetry.lock` via `poetry lock` (v2.1.3) to reflect the updated Python version constraint

**Note**: The `unittest (3.10, postgresql, stable)` failure in CI run #18 was a transient Docker timeout pulling `ghcr.io/nautobot/nautobot-dev:stable-py3.10`, not a code issue.

title: list -> iterable builtins
body:
In Python 3:
* `itertools.imap`, `itertools.ifilter`, `itertools.izip`, `dict.iterkeys`, `dict.itervalues` and `dict.iteritems` have been removed entirely
* the formerly eager (list-returning) map, filter, zip, dict.keys, dict.values and dict.items now returns lazy iterators (or view objects)

While this is not problematic when just iterating, there are numerous cases where the results are expected/used as lists which would break in P3.

=> convert all code using these functions to semantics-matching cross-version helpers (so that the behaviour is the same between P2 and P3), and ban the builtins via pylint to avoid them cropping back up.

Removed the `range` shim following discussion on `xrange` conversion commit as P3's `range` is a fairly complete Sequence object (though not a list) and `range` seems to more or less never be used for anything other than iteration, so the risk of incompatibility looks very remote.

issue #8530

body:
Bumps [python-dotenv](https://github.com/theskumar/python-dotenv) from 1.2.1 to 1.2.2.
Release notes

Sourced from python-dotenv's releases.

v1.2.2

Added

  • Support for Python 3.14, including the free-threaded (3.14t) build. (#)

Changed

  • The dotenv run command now forwards flags directly to the specified command by @​bbc2 in theskumar/python-dotenv#607
  • Improved documentation clarity regarding override behavior and the reference page.
  • Updated PyPy support to version 3.11.
  • Documentation for FIFO file support.
  • Support for Python 3.9.

Fixed

  • Improved set_key and unset_key behavior when interacting with symlinks by @​bbc2 in #790c5
  • Corrected the license specifier and added missing Python 3.14 classifiers in package metadata by @​JYOuyang in theskumar/python-dotenv#590

Breaking Changes

  • dotenv.set_key and dotenv.unset_key used to follow symlinks in some situations. This is no longer the case. For that behavior to be restored in all cases, follow_symlinks=True should be used.

  • In the CLI, set and unset used to follow symlinks in some situations. This is no longer the case.

  • dotenv.set_key, dotenv.unset_key and the CLI commands set and unset used to reset the file mode of the modified .env file to 0o600 in some situations. This is no longer the case: The original mode of the file is now preserved. Is the file needed to be created or wasn't a regular file, mode 0o600 is used.

Misc

  • skip 000 permission tests for root user by @​burnout-projects in theskumar/python-dotenv#561
  • Bump actions/checkout from 5 to 6 in the github-actions group by @​dependabot[bot] in theskumar/python-dotenv#593

title: Add deployment config, switch DB to env-driven in-memory SQLite with StaticPool, and update dependencies
body:
### Motivation
- Provide explicit Python runtime and Procfile for predictable deployment behavior and Heroku-style starts. 
- Make the database engine configurable via `DATABASE_URL` and use an in-memory SQLite default with `StaticPool` so the engine works reliably across threads (e.g. Uvicorn) and tests. 
- Align and pin dependency versions and add `greenlet` for compatibility with SQLAlchemy.

### Description
- Added `.python-version` and `runtime.txt` set to `3.12.10` and added a `Procfile` containing `web: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT`.
- Modified `app/database.py` to read `DATABASE_URL` from the environment with a default of `sqlite://`, imported `os` and `StaticPool`, and configured `create_engine` with `poolclass=StaticPool` and `connect_args={"check_same_thread": False}`.
- Updated `requirements.txt` to pin `fastapi==0.100.0`, `uvicorn==0.22.0`, `pydantic==1.10.13`, kept `sqlalchemy==2.0.36`, and added `greenlet==3.0.0`.

### Testing
- Ran the project's automated test suite with `pytest` and all tests passed.

------

title: chore: consolidate release workflow into single job
body:
## Objective
The release workflow runs Python setup and checkout twice — once for the test job and once for the publish job. This wastes CI time on duplicate environment setup for every release.

## Approach
Merge the two jobs (test → publish) into a single job that runs test → build → publish sequentially. Remove the `environment: release` setting which was not configured on PyPI trusted publisher.

## Evidence
Compared before/after workflow structure:

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Python setup calls | 2 (test job + publish job) | 1 |
| Checkout calls | 2 | 1 |
| Test gate before publish | Yes (separate job `needs`) | Yes (sequential steps) |
| Environment setting | `release` (not configured) | Removed |

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Chores**
  * Streamlined the release workflow by consolidating test and publish steps into a unified process for improved efficiency.

<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

title: Downgrade aiogram to 3.2.0 for Python 3.13 compatibility
body:
## Problem

The build fails when installing aiogram 3.3.0 on Python 3.13 because pydantic-core (a transitive dependency) errors during Rust compilation with a ForwardRef._evaluate() incompatibility introduced in Python 3.13.

## Solution

Downgraded aiogram from 3.3.0 to 3.2.0 in requirements.txt. Version 3.2.0 resolves against a compatible version of pydantic-core that builds cleanly on Python 3.13, unblocking deployment.

### Changes
- **Modified** `requirements.txt`

title: feat(quality): pre-commit hooks (husky/ruff/golangci) + SonarCloud analysis
body:
## Summary

- **Pre-commit hooks** — lint runs locally before every `git commit`, catches issues before CI
- **SonarCloud** — deep quality gate on every PR (bugs, vulnerabilities, coverage, code smells)

## What's added

### Local pre-commit (`.pre-commit-config.yaml` + husky)
| Hook | Files | What it does |
|------|-------|-------------|
| `ruff --fix` + `ruff format` | `*.py` in Python services | Auto-fix + format on save |
| `golangci-lint` + `gofmt` | `*.go` | Lint + format Go files |
| `eslint --fix` | `*.js` in Node services | Auto-fix JS issues |
| `detect-private-key` | all | Blocks accidental secret commits |
| `check-yaml` / `check-json` | all | Validates config files |

### SonarCloud (`.github/workflows/sonarcloud.yml`)
- Collects coverage from Go (`go test -coverprofile`), Python (`pytest --cov`), Node (`jest --coverage`)
- Runs SonarCloud scan with full blame history
- Quality gate blocks merge if: new bugs > 0, new vulnerabilities > 0, coverage < 80%

## Setup required (one-time manual step)
1. Go to **https://sonarcloud.io** → sign in with GitHub
2. Click **+** → Analyze new project → select `pahuldeepp/CarePulse`
3. Copy the **SONAR_TOKEN**
4. Go to GitHub repo → Settings → Secrets → Actions → New secret → `SONAR_TOKEN`

- [ ] `git commit` on a `.go` file triggers gofmt
- [ ] `git commit` on a `.js` file triggers eslint

title: Project scaffolding – Flask app factory, config, and database setup
body:
Bootstraps the entire Python project from an empty repo: Poetry project, Flask app factory, SQLite database layer, schema, smoke tests, and CI.

### Project config
- `pyproject.toml` — Poetry with `Flask ^3.1`, `APScheduler ^3.10`, `requests ^2.31`, `openai ^2.0`, `python-dotenv ^1.0`; dev deps `pytest ^8.0`, `mypy ^1.0`
- `config.py` — all settings read from env vars (`OPENAI_API_BASE`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `DATABASE_URL` → `data/metadata.db`, `SECRET_KEY`, cron intervals)
- `.env.example` — placeholder values for all required variables with inline time-unit comments; cron defaults: metadata daily (1440 min), ontologies monthly (720 h), tools weekly (168 h)

### Application factory (`app/__init__.py`)
- `create_app(config=None)` loads `config.py`, wires DB teardown, registers CLI, starts `BackgroundScheduler` (suppressed when `TESTING=True`)

### Database layer
- `app/db/sqlite.py` — uses `pathlib.Path` throughout (no `os`); `get_db()` (Flask `g`-scoped connection, works for both HTTP requests and APScheduler background jobs via `with app.app_context()`); `init_db()` (idempotent, executes `schema.sql`, not a migration tool); `close_db()` (teardown hook); no sqlite-vector extension (deferred to ontology issue)
- `app/db/schema.sql` — `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` for `users`, `sessions`, `metadata_cache`, `semantic_tools`
  - `metadata_cache` stores only URL-level crawl state (`content_hash`, `structural_summary`, `last_crawled_at`) — no `result_json`, since results are per-user (influenced by individual prompts) and live exclusively in `sessions.result_json`
- `flask db init` CLI command calls `init_db()`

### Tests (`tests/test_app.py`)
Five smoke tests covering app instantiation, config override, table creation, and idempotent `init_db()` calls — all using an in-memory SQLite database.

```python
app = create_app({"TESTING": True, "DATABASE_URL": ":memory:"})
# init_db() creates users / sessions / metadata_cache / semantic_tools
# calling init_db() twice does not raise (IF NOT EXISTS guards)
```

### CI (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`)
GitHub Actions workflow running `pytest tests/ -v` and `mypy app tests` on every push and pull request.

title: PERF: Optimize _libs.tslibs.strptime parsing
body:
- Cache iso format regex at module level instead of recompiling per call
- Use `iteritems()` instead of `iterkeys()` + redundant dict value re-lookups
- Replace locale `list.index()` with pre-built dict lookups for month/weekday names
- Remove unnecessary `date` object creation in julian day validation

```
rng = pd.date_range("2000-01-01", periods=10**6, freq="h")
strings_abbr = rng.strftime("%d/%b/%Y %H:%M:%S").tolist()

%timeit pd.to_datetime(strings_abbr, format="%d/%b/%Y %H:%M:%S")
1.02 s ± 8.98 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)  # <- PR
1.48 s ± 23.7 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)  # <- main
```

title: Bump requests from 2.32.4 to 2.33.0
body:
Bumps [requests](https://github.com/psf/requests) from 2.32.4 to 2.33.0.
Release notes

Sourced from requests's releases.

v2.33.0

2.33.0 (2026-03-25)

Announcements

  • 📣 Requests is adding inline types. If you have a typed code base that uses Requests, please take a look at #7271. Give it a try, and report any gaps or feedback you may have in the issue. 📣

Security

  • CVE-2026-25645 requests.utils.extract_zipped_paths now extracts contents to a non-deterministic location to prevent malicious file replacement. This does not affect default usage of Requests, only applications calling the utility function directly.

Improvements

  • Migrated to a PEP 517 build system using setuptools. (#7012)

Bugfixes

  • Fixed an issue where an empty netrc entry could cause malformed authentication to be applied to Requests on Python 3.11+. (#7205)

Deprecations

  • Dropped support for Python 3.9 following its end of support. (#7196)

Documentation

  • Various typo fixes and doc improvements.

New Contributors

  • @​M0d3v1 made their first contribution in psf/requests#6865
  • @​aminvakil made their first contribution in psf/requests#7220
  • @​E8Price made their first contribution in psf/requests#6960
  • @​mitre88 made their first contribution in psf/requests#7244
  • @​magsen made their first contribution in psf/requests#6553
  • @​Rohan5commit made their first contribution in psf/requests#7227

Full Changelog: https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/main/HISTORY.md#2330-2026-03-25

v2.32.5

2.32.5 (2025-08-18)

Bugfixes

  • The SSLContext caching feature originally introduced in 2.32.0 has created a new class of issues in Requests that have had negative impact across a number of use cases. The Requests team has decided to revert this feature as long term maintenance of it is proving to be unsustainable in its current iteration.

Deprecations

  • Added support for Python 3.14.
  • Dropped support for Python 3.8 following its end of support.
Changelog

Sourced from requests's changelog.

2.33.0 (2026-03-25)

Announcements

  • 📣 Requests is adding inline types. If you have a typed code base that uses Requests, please take a look at #7271. Give it a try, and report any gaps or feedback you may have in the issue. 📣

Security

  • CVE-2026-25645 requests.utils.extract_zipped_paths now extracts contents to a non-deterministic location to prevent malicious file replacement. This does not affect default usage of Requests, only applications calling the utility function directly.

Improvements

  • Migrated to a PEP 517 build system using setuptools. (#7012)

Bugfixes

  • Fixed an issue where an empty netrc entry could cause malformed authentication to be applied to Requests on Python 3.11+. (#7205)

Deprecations

  • Dropped support for Python 3.9 following its end of support. (#7196)

Documentation

  • Various typo fixes and doc improvements.

2.32.5 (2025-08-18)

Bugfixes

  • The SSLContext caching feature originally introduced in 2.32.0 has created a new class of issues in Requests that have had negative impact across a number of use cases. The Requests team has decided to revert this feature as long term maintenance of it is proving to be unsustainable in its current iteration.

Deprecations

  • Added support for Python 3.14.
  • Dropped support for Python 3.8 following its end of support.

title: feat: migrate DoltgreSQL to Dolt MySQL
body:
## Summary

Migrate acme-health from DoltgreSQL (PostgreSQL wire protocol, beta) to Dolt MySQL (MySQL wire protocol, mature). Smoke testing the original DoltgreSQL setup hit three blockers: no SQL isolation levels, missing `EXCLUDED` keyword for UPSERTs, and underdocumented environment variables. Dolt's MySQL wire has been stable since 2019 and has native .NET Aspire integration via `AddMySql`.

17 files changed across 5 commits. All tests pass (1 C# unit + 29 Python).

## Changes

**Phase 1 — AppHost Infrastructure** (`db58af0`)
- `AddContainer("doltgresql")` → `AddMySql("dolt").WithImage("dolthub/dolt-sql-server", "v1.50.2")`
- `AddParameter("dolt-password", secret: true)` with User Secrets for credential management
- `AddDatabase("acme-health")` for auto DB creation
- Manual `ReferenceExpression` replaced with native `WithReference(db)` on all 4 services
- `DOLT_ROOT_HOST=%` for Docker network access (Dolt v1.46+ security)
- Python endpoint fix: `WithEndpoint` replaces `WithHttpEndpoint` (aspire#14408)

**Phase 2 — FhirIngest MySQL Migration** (`b968c22`)
- Npgsql 9.0.3 → MySqlConnector 2.4.0
- `$N` positional params → `@name` named params
- Row alias UPSERT syntax (`AS new ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE col = new.col`)
- Schema DDL: `TEXT PRIMARY KEY` → `VARCHAR(255)`, inline FK → `CONSTRAINT FOREIGN KEY`
- `DOLT_COMMIT` param: `$1` → `@msg`

**Phase 3 — Wearable Normalizer MySQL Migration** (`adf0a9b`)
- psycopg → aiomysql
- `parse_connection_string()` returns `dict` (aiomysql kwargs) instead of DSN string
- aiomysql connection pattern: explicit `connect(**kwargs)` / `commit()` / `close()`
- DDL: `SERIAL` → `INT AUTO_INCREMENT`
- clinical-extractor dependency swapped (stub service, no logic changes)

**Phase 4 — Documentation** (`12eaec4`)
- ADR-1001 superseded with migration rationale (3 blocking DoltgreSQL gaps)
- README architecture diagram, technology table, driver names updated
- Sequence diagrams: participant labels changed

**CR Polish** (`9261b07`)
- Removed hardcoded password from appsettings.json → User Secrets
- Replaced deprecated `VALUES()` with row alias syntax for MySQL 8.0.20+ forward compat
- Restructured `create_schema()` for cleaner connection cleanup
- Added user-secrets setup step to README

## Quality Evidence

| Phase | Review Score | Challenger | Tests |
|-------|-------------|------------|-------|
| 1: AppHost | 9.3/10 | 5 approved, 0 challenges | `dotnet build` clean |
| 2: FhirIngest | 9.1/10 | 10 approved, 0 challenges | 1 unit pass |
| 3: Wearable | 9.3/10 | 12 approved, 0 challenges | 29/29 pytest pass |
| 4: Docs | 9.5/10 | — | grep validation |
| CR Polish | — | — | all tests re-verified |

**CodeRabbit CLI**: 4 findings identified and resolved before PR creation.

## Testing

- [x] `dotnet build AcmeHealth.slnx` — 0 errors, 0 warnings
- [x] `dotnet test --filter "Category!=Integration"` — 1 passed
- [x] `uv run pytest` (wearable-normalizer) — 29 passed
- [ ] End-to-end with Docker — deferred to smoke test (acme-svu.1)

## Review Guidance

| Priority | Files | Why |
|----------|-------|-----|
| High | `src/AppHost/AppHost.cs` | Aspire orchestration — all service wiring |
| High | `src/Acme.Stack.FhirIngest/Program.cs` | Largest code change — SQL dialect, params |
| Medium | `src/services/wearable-normalizer/main.py` | Python driver swap, connection parser |
| Medium | `src/Acme.Stack.Core/Models.cs` | Shared schema DDL |
| Low | `tests/`, `docs/`, `README.md` | Mechanical updates |

## Security

| Concern | Mitigation |
|---------|------------|
| Database credentials | `AddParameter(secret: true)` + `dotnet user-secrets` — no secrets in source |
| Docker network | `DOLT_ROOT_HOST=%` scoped to container network only |

## Spec

Driven by [dolt-mysql-migration](https://github.com/peterkc/acme-sdk/tree/specs/dolt-mysql-migration) — 5 phases, consensus score 9.0/10 from triple-model evaluation.


<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Chores**
  * Migrated database platform from PostgreSQL/DoltgreSQL to MySQL/Dolt with schema and type adjustments for MySQL compatibility.
  * Replaced DB drivers and updated connection handling, parameterization, and commit/versioning to MySQL semantics.
  * Updated container/service orchestration to use a MySQL-based Dolt instance with configurable password and database reference wiring.
  * Aligned runtime configs, tests, and dependency manifests to MySQL/aiomysql connection formats and behaviors.

title: Create faithful Python port of C inference engine, reorganize versions, update README
body:
The Python version (`postgpt_q.py`) diverged significantly from the canonical C implementation — different Dario coefficients, missing best-of-3 generation, no Schumann resonance, no memory persistence, dynamic chamber modulation instead of static, DOE alpha 2× stronger. Created a faithful 1:1 Python port and preserved the research version separately.

### Changes

- **`postgpt_q.py` → `qresearch.py`** — old research/training version (Val autograd, RoPE, wormholes, interference, dynamic Dario modulation) preserved under new name
- **New `postgpt_q.py`** (1449 lines) — faithful port of `postgpt_q.c`:
  - Same constants (`DOE_ALPHA=0.05`, `TOP_K=15`, `QPTQ_MAGIC=0x51505451`)
  - Same static Dario coefficients (`heb=0.4/0.8`, `bg=5.0/15.0` with/without weights)
  - Same pipeline: best-of-3 coherence scoring, greedy→nucleus hybrid, age-based repetition penalty, bigram blocking, Schumann resonance, `q.memory` persistence
  - No training, no autograd, no RoPE — pure inference matching C
- **`assets/`** — browser screenshots of `q.html` (start, generation, field view)
- **`README.md`** rewritten:
  - Author credit, versions table with canonical status
  - Feature comparison table (canonical vs research)
  - Inline browser screenshots
  - `.gitignore` updated for `__pycache__`

### Key differences resolved (C canonical → Python port)

| Feature | Old `postgpt_q.py` | New (matches C) |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Dario bigram coeff | 12.0 fixed | 5.0/15.0 (tf/no-tf) |
| DOE alpha | 0.1 | 0.05 |
| Best-of-3 | ❌ | ✅ |
| Schumann resonance | ❌ | ✅ |
| Memory persistence | ❌ | ✅ q.memory |
| Rep penalty | uniform 0.5 | age-based 0.3+0.035×age |

### Browser version screenshots

title: Introduce application ports and strong-typed feature config plumbing
body:
### Motivation
- Decouple feature apps from the concrete `MainConfigLoader` and centralize feature input shape by introducing explicit application ports and typed config objects to simplify downstream code and validations.
- Normalize how approximation/random-matrix/graph settings are represented and consumed so feature apps only rely on well-typed inputs instead of raw toml dicts.

### Description
- Add `src/monocycle_nash/application_ports.py` with `FeatureWorkflowInputPort` protocol and frozen dataclasses `LoadedFeatureInputs`, `ApproximationConfig`, `RandomMatrixConfig`, `PayoffGraphConfig`, and `CharacterGraphConfig` to represent feature inputs.
- Update feature entrypoints (`compare_approximation`, `compare_random_approximation`, `compare_payoff`, `solve_payoff`, `graph_payoff`, `plot_characters`) to accept `FeatureWorkflowInputPort` and to use the new typed config fields instead of raw dicts, and to write `raw_input` slices when snapshotting.
- Simplify approximation/distance builders in `compare_approximation_app.py` to accept algorithm names and remove legacy resolution helpers; adjust `compare_random_approximation_app.py` to parse `RandomMatrixConfig` instead of raw dicts and to build acceptance conditions from the typed field.
- Revamp `MainConfigLoader` to construct `LoadedFeatureInputs` with typed graph/approximation/random-matrix configs, add `load_matrix_data` helper, and centralize validation and parsing helpers (`_build_graph_config`, `_build_approximation_config`, `_build_random_matrix_config`, etc.).
- Update tests in `tests/loader/test_main_config.py` to assert against the new typed config fields.

### Testing
- Ran loader unit tests `pytest tests/loader/test_main_config.py` and assertions targeting the new `MainConfigLoader` behavior passed.
- Ran the project test subset with `pytest -q` covering modified modules and observed no failures.

------
[Codex Task](https://chatgpt.com/codex/tasks/task_e_69c49127e0bc83309d838dafa151e3a9)

title: Refactor dict-based runtime/config IO into explicit RuntimeSetting and typed approximation configs
body:
### Motivation
- The code previously passed around raw `dict` objects for runtime settings and approximation inputs, making it hard to know which fields are used and encouraging bad practices. 
- The goal was to make IO/config interfaces explicit at the domain/core layer and remove unused dict-based plumbing so callers rely on typed fields instead of arbitrary keys.

### Description
- Introduced a typed runtime model and parser abstraction in `runmeta/setting_domain.py` with `RuntimeSetting`, `RunMetaSetting`, `OutputSetting`, `AnalysisProjectSetting`, and `RuntimeSettingParser` to represent settings explicitly. 
- Reworked `loader/runtime_common.py` to accept and return `RuntimeSetting` objects, added `TomlRuntimeSettingParser`, updated `load_inputs`, `prepare_run_session`, `write_input_snapshots`, and related helpers to use the typed model and to serialize snapshots from explicit fields. 
- Replaced dict-based approximation and random-matrix inputs with typed models in `approximation/infra.py` (removed `raw_input`, added `dominant_eigen_ratio_bin_edges` etc.), adjusted apps in `approximation/compare_*.py` to emit TOML payloads built from those fields, and updated `matrix/approximation.py` to accept explicit `dominant_eigen_ratio_bin_edges` in `quality_parameters`. 
- Removed dead `application_ports.py` and updated feature infrastructures (`equilibrium/infra.py`, `graph/infra.py`, `approximation/infra.py`) and tests to parse settings via the new parser and pass typed settings through the application boundary. 

### Testing
- Ran the full test suite with `uv run pytest`, which initially revealed one failing test due to the changed `quality_parameters` signature. 
- After updating the test and implementing the typed interfaces, re-ran `uv run pytest` and verified the full suite passes: `258 passed`.

------
[Codex Task](https://chatgpt.com/codex/tasks/task_e_69c4b040a06c83308de6b3379d3a8e4f)

Add per-round user judgements and an awareness score (1–10) with tailored feedback, computed after the verdict and displayed in the courtroom UI. Helps users learn to spot fraud indicators with specific, encouraging guidance.

- **New Features**
  - Backend: New POST /api/trial/{case_id}/judgement to record per-round judgements (validated, invalid coerced to "neutral"); TrialState extended with user_judgements and awareness_score_result; awareness_scorer agent generates JSON score + feedback (clamped 1–10, safe fallback) and emits an awareness_score SSE event; workflow inserts awareness_scorer after score_calculator.
  - Frontend: Judgement overlay prompts after each round; AwarenessScore component shows the final score and feedback; removed the "Red Flags" education section from the verdict view.
  - Tests: Added unit tests covering scoring behavior, rounding, no-judgement handling, and value validation.

<sup>Written for commit bcf313d454bb0ec6c5b1ea041a18ca7f12f059b8. Summary will update on new commits.</sup>

<!-- End of auto-generated description by cubic. -->

title: Break apart install script
body:
<!-- This is an auto-generated description by cubic. -->
## Summary by cubic
Split the Bash installer into a thin bootstrap and a Python-based installer for clearer flow, better UX, and testability. Users keep the same flags and get a safer CLI install, improved prompts, Docker Compose v1/v2 support, and reliable port 25 reachability checks with cloud guidance.

- **Refactors**
  - `install.sh` now handles Docker, firewall, repo clone, Python check, then execs `python3 -m installer.main` (forwards all flags; only `--dir` parsed in Bash; install dir passed via `PRIMITIVEMAIL_DIR`).
  - Moved logic into `installer/config.py`, `installer/server.py`, and `installer/ui.py` (TTY-safe prompts via `/dev/tty`, spinner with tail-on-error, build/start, port 25 checks with cloud hints and local fallback, and `docker compose`/`docker-compose` detection).
  - Pure functions for `.env` generation, summaries, DNS/next steps, and spoof-protection validation; `.env` is written with 0600 perms.

- **New Features**
  - Auto-install Python 3 on `apt`/`yum`/`dnf` systems; install URL updated to `https://get.primitive.dev`.
  - CLI installs to `~/.local/bin`, ensures PATH in `.bashrc`/`.profile`/`.zshrc`, and best-effort symlink to `/usr/local/bin/primitive` (no-prompt sudo if available).
  - Added tests in `test_installer.py` covering env/config generation, IP detection, spoof protection, and DNS/next-steps summaries.

<sup>Written for commit d66c1c7ffef965f8e492497cf0918a8cd8034c89. Summary will update on new commits.</sup>

<!-- End of auto-generated description by cubic. -->

title: Add /healthz endpoint and stabilize Render start/health check
body:
### Motivation
- Prevent deploy-healthcheck failures and environment drift by providing a lightweight health endpoint and ensuring the app binds the platform-assigned port reliably.

### Description
- Add a `GET /healthz` endpoint in `main.py` that returns `{"status": "ok"}` for fast health probes.
- Update `render.yaml` to run Uvicorn using the platform port (`$PORT`) and change `healthCheckPath` to `/healthz`.
- Pin Render Python runtime by adding `PYTHON_VERSION: 3.11.11` to `render.yaml` to reduce runtime drift.

### Testing
- Ran `python -m py_compile main.py` which succeeded.
- Launched the app locally with Uvicorn and verified `GET /healthz` returned `{"status":"ok"}` using `curl`, which succeeded.
- Confirmed the server responds on the configured port when started with the updated `startCommand`.

title: Add auto-discovery system: eliminate hardcoded backend IP in ESP32 firmware
body:
ESP32 firmware had a hardcoded backend IP (`10.83.60.246`) requiring a reflash on every laptop IP change. This adds a 4-layer runtime discovery system so the device resolves the backend automatically on boot.

## Discovery flow (ESP32, ordered by priority)

1. **mDNS** — resolve `chetan-robot.local` (3 retries)
2. **UDP broadcast** — probe `255.255.255.255:54321`, parse `CHETAN_ROBOT_BACKEND:<ip>:<port>` reply (2 retries)
3. **NVS/EEPROM** — try last-saved IP (persisted by whichever method succeeded previously)
4. **AP mode fallback** — creates `ChetanRobot_SETUP` hotspot + captive portal at `192.168.4.1`; user submits backend IP via browser; reboots into normal flow

Each resolved address is verified against `/health` before being accepted and saved to NVS.

## Backend changes
- `app/discovery/mdns_advertiser.py` — registers service as `chetan-robot._http._tcp.local.` via `zeroconf`
- `app/discovery/udp_broadcaster.py` — async UDP listener on port `54321` responding to discovery probes
- `app/main.py` lifespan — starts/stops both services alongside existing monitors
- `requirements.txt` — adds `zeroconf==0.131.0`

## ESP32 firmware changes (`espcod/ESPCAM/`)
- `discovery.h` — orchestrator; calls all four layers in sequence
- `mdns_handler.h` — ESPmDNS service query + hostname fallback
- `udp_discovery.h` — broadcast probe + response parser
- `eeprom_config.h` — NVS read/write via `Preferences`
- `ap_mode.h` — softAP + DNS captive redirect + `WebServer` config form
- `ESPCAM.ino` — removes hardcoded IP/device-ID; calls `discover_backend()` post-WiFi; device ID now derived from MAC (`espcam_XXYYZZ`); fixes `is_last` bitwise-OR bug (`|` → `.as<bool>()`)

```cpp
// Before
const char BACKEND_HOST[] = "10.83.60.246";
const char DEVICE_ID[]    = "camcontroller";

// After — resolved at runtime
char     BACKEND_HOST[64] = "";
uint16_t BACKEND_PORT     = 8000;
char     DEVICE_ID[32]    = "espcam";  // populated from MAC in setup()

// In setup(), after WiFi connects:
discover_backend(BACKEND_HOST, sizeof(BACKEND_HOST), &BACKEND_PORT);
```

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

# Auto-Discovery WiFi & mDNS System for ESP32-Backend Connection

## Problem
Currently, the system requires:
1. Hardcoded backend IP address in ESPCAM.ino (line 11: `const char BACKEND_HOST[] = "10.83.60.246"`)
2. Manual IP update every time laptop IP changes
3. Dependency on external hotspot/WiFi network
4. No device discovery mechanism

## Solution Requirements
Implement a **multi-layered auto-discovery system** without external dependencies:

### Option 1: mDNS (Multicast DNS) - Primary Solution
- Backend advertises as `chetan-robot.local` over mDNS
- ESP32 discovers backend using `chetan_robot.local` hostname
- Works on any WiFi network (or direct AP mode)
- Zero configuration needed after setup

### Option 2: Broadcast UDP Discovery - Fallback
- ESP32 broadcasts UDP discovery request on local network
- Backend listens and responds with its IP:port
- Auto-connects to discovered backend
- Works within LAN segments

### Option 3: Direct AP Mode + Captive Portal
- ESP32 creates WiFi hotspot if backend not found
- User connects to ESP32's AP (e.g., "ChetanRobot_SETUP")
- Captive portal shows available backends on network
- User selects backend to connect

### Option 4: Configuration Portal + EEPROM
- ESP32 boots into AP mode first time
- Stores backend hostname/IP in EEPROM
- Reconnects using stored config on reboot
- User can trigger reconfig via GPIO button or command

## Implementation Tasks

### Task 1: Backend mDNS Advertising (Python FastAPI)
- Install `zeroconf` package
- Advertise FastAPI service as `_http._tcp` with hostname `chetan-robot`
- Include service info: port, device list, etc.

### Task 2: ESP32 mDNS Discovery + Connection
- Use ESP-IDF mDNS client to discover `chetan-robot.local`
- Implement fallback to broadcast UDP if mDNS fails
- Add retry mechanism with exponential backoff
- Store discovered IP temporarily

### Task 3: Broadcast UDP Discovery Service
- Implement UDP listener on backend port 5353 (or custom)
- Listen for discovery requests from ESP32
- Respond with backend IP:port
- Add to both firmware and backend

### Task 4: Fallback AP Mode + Web Configuration
- If backend not found within 30 seconds, create WiFi AP
- Implement captive portal showing network scan results
- Allow user to input custom IP or hostname
- Save to EEPROM for next boot

### Task 5: Configuration Persistence
- Add EEPROM storage for backend hostname/IP
- Implement reset mechanism (button press or command)
- Store WiFi credentials securely
- Add configuration validation

### Task 6: Testing & Documentation
- Test all 4 discovery methods
- Test failover scenarios
- Document setup process
- Add troubleshooting guide

## Architecture Diagram
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CONNECTION FLOW                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                           │
│  ESP32 Boot                                              │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  [Method 1] Try mDNS Discovery (chetan-robot.local)     │
│     ├─ Success → Store IP, Connect ✓                    │
│     └─ Fail → Retry 3x                                  │
│                                                           │
│  [Method 2] Try UDP Broadcast Discovery                 │
│     ├─ Success → Store IP, Connect ✓                    │
│     └─ Fail → Retry 2x                                  │
│                                                           │
│  [Method 3] Try Stored IP from EEPROM                   │
│     ├─ Success → Connect ✓                              │
│     └─ Fail → Go to Method 4                            │
│                                                           │
│  [Method 4] Enter AP Mode + Captive Portal              │
│     ├─ Show network list                                │
│     ├─ User selects backend                             │
│     └─ Save & Reboot → Back to Method 1                │
│                                                           │
│  Connected to Backend ✓                                  │
│     ↓                                                     │
│  WebSocket Registration, Audio Processing, etc.         │
│                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## File Structure
```
espcod/
├── ESPCAM/
│   ├── ESPCAM.ino (updated)
│   ├── discovery.h (new)
│   ├── mdns_handler.h (new)
│   ├── udp_discovery.h (new)
│   ├── ap_mode.h (new)
│   └── eeprom_config.h (new)

amhrpd-backend/
├── app/
│   ├── main.py (updated)
│   ├── discovery/ (new)
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── mdns_advertiser.py
│   │   └── udp_broadcaster.py
│   └── config.py (updated)
```

## Expected Behavior After Implementation

### Scenario 1: Same WiFi Network (Ideal)
```
1. ESP32 powers on
2. Scans for mDNS service "chetan-robot.local"
3. Found! Connects to backend
4. Success ✓...

</details>

title: chore: Update dependencies
fix(vercel): Align `dump_messages` output with Vercel spec
body:
<!-- Thank you for contributing to Pydantic AI! -->

<!-- Please add the issue number that should be closed when this PR is merged. -->
<!-- If there is no issue, please create one first, even for simple bug fixes. -->

- Closes #4193
- Closes #4263

### Pre-Review Checklist

<!-- These checks need to be completed before a PR is reviewed, -->
<!-- but you can submit a draft early to indicate that the issue is being worked on. -->

- [x] Any **AI generated code** has been reviewed line-by-line by the human PR author, who stands by it.
- [ ] Possible breaking changes, documented below
- [x] **Linting and type checking** pass per `make format` and `make typecheck`.
- [x] **PR title** is fit for the [release changelog](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/releases).

### Pre-Merge Checklist

<!-- These checks need to be completed before a PR is merged, -->
<!-- but as PRs often change significantly during review, -->
<!-- it's OK for them to be incomplete when review is first requested. -->

- [x] Test section filled out below, no new tests added
- [x] No new docs needed

---

### The Problem

`dump_messages()` was outputting tool parts differently from the event stream, causing a format mismatch:

**Before (incorrect):**
```json
{
  "type": "dynamic-tool",
  "toolName": "search",
  "input": "{\"q\":\"test\"}",
  "output": "{\"r\":1}"
}
```

**After (correct):**
```json
{
  "type": "tool-search",
  "input": {"q": "test"},
  "output": {"r": 1}
}
```

This broke round-trip compatibility: streaming chunks worked correctly, but saved messages failed when reloaded via `load_messages()`.

### Background: Tool Types in Vercel AI

The Vercel AI adapter handles two categories of tools:

1. **Builtin tools** (`BuiltinToolCallPart`/`BuiltinToolReturnPart`) - Provider-executed tools like OpenAI's `web_search_preview`. These use `provider_executed=True` and the `type` field contains the tool name directly (e.g., `tool-web_search`).

2. **Dynamic tools** (`ToolCallPart`/`ToolReturnPart`) - User-defined tools executed by Pydantic AI. These use `provider_executed=False`.

Both categories should use the same output format: `type: 'tool-{name}'` with parsed dict objects for `input`/`output`.

The codebase had separate part types (`DynamicToolOutputAvailablePart`, etc.) that used a different format with `type: 'dynamic-tool'` and a separate `tool_name` field. These were only used by `dump_messages()`, not the event stream.

### Changes

**`_adapter.py`:**
- Changed dynamic tool output to use `ToolOutputAvailablePart`/`ToolInputAvailablePart`/`ToolOutputErrorPart` (same as builtin tools)
- Changed `type` field from `'dynamic-tool'` to `f'tool-{part.tool_name}'`
- Changed `input` from `args_as_json_str()` (JSON string) to `args_as_dict()` (parsed dict)
- Changed `output` from `model_response_str()` (JSON string) to unwrapped `model_response_object()` (parsed dict)
- Added `provider_executed=False` to distinguish from builtin tools
- Removed unused `DynamicTool*Part` imports

**Key detail:** The `model_response_object()` method wraps non-dict values in `{'return_value': ...}` for Gemini compatibility. We unwrap this before outputting (matching the event stream's `_tool_return_output` helper) since `return_value` is not part of the Vercel spec.

### Backwards Compatibility

**Reading old data:** ✅ Fully compatible. `load_messages()` already handles both formats:
- Accepts both `DynamicToolUIPart` (`type='dynamic-tool'`) and `ToolUIPart` (`type='tool-{name}'`)
- Parses `input` whether it's a JSON string or dict

**Writing new data:** ⚠️ Format change. `dump_messages()` now outputs:
- `type: 'tool-{name}'` instead of `type: 'dynamic-tool'` with separate `tool_name`
- `input`/`output` as parsed dicts instead of JSON strings
- New `provider_executed: false` field

**Who might be affected:**
- External code parsing `dump_messages()` output and expecting `type === 'dynamic-tool'` or `typeof input === 'string'`
- Vercel AI SDK frontends should be unaffected since streaming events already used the correct format - this change makes `dump_messages()` consistent with streaming

### Test plan

- [x] All existing `dump_messages` tests updated and passing
- [x] Round-trip test (`dump_messages` -> `load_messages`) now passes
- [x] Format matches event stream output

title: plot_channel_contribution_grid to MMM Multidimensional + Documentation Notebook
body:
Add `plot_channel_contribution_grid` to MMM Multidimensional for counterfactual spend analysis

## PR Description

### Summary

This PR adds the `plot_channel_contribution_grid` method and supporting infrastructure to the `MMM` class in `pymc_marketing.mmm.multidimensional`, enabling users to visualize how channel contributions change across different spend levels through counterfactual analysis.

### What's New

#### New Methods in `MMM` (multidimensional.py)

1. **`get_channel_contribution_forward_pass_grid`** - Computes channel contributions for a grid of spend multipliers (deltas). This method:
   - Takes `start`, `stop`, and `num` parameters to define a multiplier grid (e.g., 0x to 2x current spend)
   - Scales the original channel data by each delta value
   - Uses `sample_posterior_predictive` to compute contributions for each scenario
   - Returns an `xr.DataArray` with dims `(delta, chain, draw, date, *dims, channel)`

2. **`plot_channel_contribution_grid`** - High-level plotting method that:
   - Computes the contribution grid internally
   - Supports `absolute_xrange` to show absolute spend values instead of multipliers
   - Supports `dims` filtering (e.g., `{"country": "US"}`)
   - Supports `aggregation` operations (e.g., `{"sum": ("country",)}`)
   - Delegates visualization to the plot suite

3. **`_build_prediction_dataframe`** - Helper method to convert channel xarray data to DataFrame format for `sample_posterior_predictive`

#### New Method in `MMMPlotSuite` (plot.py)

- **`channel_contribution_grid`** - Visualization method that:
  - Plots mean contribution and HDI bands for each channel across delta values
  - Handles dimension filtering and aggregation
  - Creates subplots for each dimension combination
  - Adds reference lines (δ=1 for relative, current spend for absolute)

### New Tutorial Notebook

Added `docs/source/notebooks/mmm/media_saturation.ipynb` - A comprehensive tutorial that:
- Explains the difference between **direct/marginal contribution** (`saturation_scatterplot`) and **total contribution over spend share** (`plot_channel_contribution_grid`)
- Clarifies a common source of confusion: these visualizations answer different questions
- Demonstrates basic and advanced usage including dimension filtering and aggregation
- Provides side-by-side comparison of both visualization approaches

### Key Distinction Explained in Tutorial

| Aspect | `saturation_scatterplot` | `plot_channel_contribution_grid` |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **X-axis** | Spend at each time point | Spend multiplier or total spend |
| **Y-axis** | Direct contribution per time point | Total contribution summed over time |
| **Question answered** | What is the marginal contribution at each spend level? | What happens to total impact if I change budget? |

### Tests

Added comprehensive test coverage in `tests/mmm/test_multidimensional.py`:
- `TestGetChannelContributionForwardPassGrid` - Tests for grid computation, shape validation, error handling, and multi-dimensional support
- `TestPlotChannelContributionGrid` - Tests for basic plotting, absolute x-range, dimension filtering, and aggregation

### Gallery Update

Added the new media saturation tutorial to the documentation gallery.
----
📚 Documentation preview 📚: https://pymc-marketing--2177.org.readthedocs.build/en/2177/

<!-- readthedocs-preview pymc-marketing end -->

title: Drop Python 2.7 support and modernize codebase
body:
### TL;DR

Drop Python 2.7 support and modernize the codebase to require Python 3.8+

### What changed?

- Removed Python 2.7 from CI test matrices in both GitHub Actions workflows
- Upgraded GitHub Actions from v2 to v4 for artifact upload/download
- Replaced `pkg_resources` with `importlib.metadata` for version detection
- Removed Python 2/3 compatibility code including `izip`, `basestring`, and `PY2`/`PY3` checks
- Simplified async support by making `RtlSdrAio` the default `RtlSdr` class
- Removed conditional librtlsdr building and Python 2-specific dependencies
- Updated package classifiers and added `python_requires = >=3.8` in setup.cfg
- Removed Python version checks from test configuration

### How to test?

Run the existing test suite on Python 3.8+ to ensure all functionality works correctly. The CI workflows will now only test against Python 3.8, 3.9, 3.10, and 3.11 on all supported platforms.

### Why make this change?

Python 2.7 reached end-of-life in January 2020 and is no longer supported. This modernization removes legacy compatibility code, simplifies the codebase, and allows the project to take advantage of modern Python features while reducing maintenance burden.

title: gh-137821: Convert _json module to use Argument Clinic
body:
<!--
Thanks for your contribution!
Please read this comment in its entirety. It's quite important.

# Pull Request title

It should be in the following format:

```
gh-NNNNNN: Summary of the changes made
```

Where: gh-NNNNNN refers to the GitHub issue number.

Most PRs will require an issue number. Trivial changes, like fixing a typo, do not need an issue.

# Backport Pull Request title

If this is a backport PR (PR made against branches other than `main`),
please ensure that the PR title is in the following format:

```
[X.Y] <title from the original PR> (GH-NNNNNN)
```

Where: [X.Y] is the branch name, for example: [3.13].

GH-NNNNNN refers to the PR number from `main`.

-->

title: Add SSM dynamics (S4, S5, Mamba) and drop-in SSMDynamics for TD-MPC2
body:
### Motivation
- Provide Phase 2 structured state-space world models (S4, S5, Mamba) so the TD-MPC2 dynamics can be swapped from an MLP to an SSM for stability, long-range memory, and rollout-error evaluation. 
- Expose the SSM variants as a drop-in replacement so downstream planning and Phase 3 work (MPPI, SimNorm, SAM) can be applied without changing trainer logic.

### Description
- Added an `ssm/` package with `s5_layer.py`, `s4_layer.py`, `mamba_layer.py`, and `ssm_world_model.py` implementing diagonal/stable recurrences and a `SSMDynamics` wrapper that projects state to latent space and manages hidden state via `reset_hidden` and `forward(z, a)`.
- Extended `TDMPC2Model` (`tdmpc2/model.py`) to accept `dynamics_type` and `ssm_state_dim`, instantiate either `MLPDynamics` or `SSMDynamics`, and reset SSM hidden state at the start of rollouts.
- Added rollout-error diagnostics to the trainer (`tdmpc2/trainer.py`) with new config fields `rollout_error_horizon` and `rollout_error_every_steps` and a helper `_compute_rollout_error_metric` that logs `rollout_error/h{H}` into metrics.
- Extended the CLI and entrypoints by adding `--dynamics-type` and `--ssm-state-dim` to `tdmpc2/train_tdmpc2.py`, created `train_tdmpc2_s4.py`, `train_tdmpc2_s5.py`, and `train_tdmpc2_mamba.py`, and exposed `tdmpc-s4`, `tdmpc-s5`, and `tdmpc-mamba` commands in `main.py`.

### Testing
- Compiled the codebase with `python -m compileall main.py tdmpc2 ssm train_tdmpc2_s4.py train_tdmpc2_s5.py train_tdmpc2_mamba.py`, which succeeded without syntax errors.
- Attempted a small runtime smoke test that constructs `TDMPC2Model` for each dynamics type and runs `rollout`, but this execution failed due to the environment missing `torch` (`ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'torch'`), so runtime validation could not be completed here.
- No other automated tests were added; existing training entrypoints and metrics logging were exercised via static checks and imports.

------
[Codex Task](https://chatgpt.com/codex/tasks/task_e_69d4d95f0354832e8b4fed5dcd5993c5)

title: v2: decision-engine statusbar rewrite
body:
## Summary
- Complete rewrite from 4 bash scripts (~600 LOC) → single Python file (zero external deps)
- Adds burn rate prediction via linear regression — warns you before you hit rate limits
- Runway alert only appears when you'll exhaust quota before it resets
- Context velocity arrows show how fast your context window is filling
- Peak hours indicator for Anthropic's 2x consumption window
- Truecolor gradient bars with sub-character Unicode block precision
- Responsive layout drops low-priority widgets to fit terminal width
- Removes jq and bun/ccusage dependencies entirely

## What it looks like
```
S ████████▍─ 73% ↻1h08m 12%/h │ W █████████▊ 92% ↻Mon 5pm │ Ctx 45%↑ │ $2.71 │ opus-4.6 H │ ⚡Peak │ main*2
```

## Test plan
- [x] `--demo` renders gradient bars at all percentages
- [x] `--config` shows all settings
- [x] `--help` shows usage
- [x] Simulated stdin JSON renders full status line
- [x] Empty stdin gracefully falls back to "Claude"
- [x] Garbage stdin gracefully falls back to "Claude"
- [x] Empty JSON `{}` gracefully falls back to "Claude"

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

  * Compact, responsive status bar with burn-rate runway prediction, context-velocity arrows, peak-hour alerts, adaptive layout, and configurable widgets (session/weekly/context/cost/model/git/effort).

  * README rewritten with streamlined install flow, concise CLI usage examples, widget list, and updated requirements.

title: Migrate to psycopg 3.x for Python 3.13 compatibility
body:
`psycopg2-binary` lacks Python 3.13 support, breaking Streamlit Cloud deployments.

## Changes

**Dependencies**
- `requirements.txt`: Replace `psycopg2-binary==2.9.9` → `psycopg[binary]==3.1.18`

**SQLAlchemy Dialect**
- `backend/app/core/config.py`: Update connection URL to use explicit driver dialect
```python
# Before
return f"postgresql://{self.POSTGRES_USER}:..."

# After  
return f"postgresql+psycopg://{self.POSTGRES_USER}:..."
```

**Import Path**
- `backend/app/db/database.py`: Fix relative import `from .config` → `from ..core.config`

**Docker Images**
- `deployment/docker/Dockerfile.{backend,frontend}`: Bump base image `python:3.11-slim` → `python:3.13-slim`

**Documentation**
- `README.md`: Note Python 3.13 support, update setup instructions

## Compatibility

Maintains backward compatibility with Python 3.11+. No API changes.

title: Migrate to async SQLAlchemy 2.0
body:
## Summary

- Migrated entire DB layer from synchronous SQLAlchemy (`create_engine` + `Session`) to async SQLAlchemy 2.0 (`create_async_engine` + `AsyncSession`)
- All ~60 route handlers, ~30 service functions, and 6 background pollers now use non-blocking async DB I/O
- Eliminates event loop starvation that caused 15-20 second intermittent page load delays (7 pollers were executing 10+ blocking DB calls/sec on a single-threaded uvicorn worker)

## Changes (31 files, +637/-608)

**Phase 0**: Converted 5 legacy `db.query().delete()` calls to SA 2.0 `delete()` statements; added `aiosqlite` dep

**Phase 1**: New async engine + session factory in `app/db.py` with `expire_on_commit=False`

**Phase 2**: Converted all route handlers and service functions — mechanical `db.scalars()` → `await db.scalars()` transformation across 15 files

**Phase 3**: Replaced `asyncio.to_thread(SessionLocal())` pattern in all 6 pollers with native `async with AsyncSessionLocal()`

**Phase 4**: Converted 3 test files to `IsolatedAsyncioTestCase` with `aiosqlite`

**Phase 5**: Removed sync `engine` and `SessionLocal` from `app/db.py`; converted startup `create_all` to async

**Alembic**: Untouched — creates its own sync engine via `engine_from_config()`, imports only `DATABASE_URL` and `Base`

## Test plan

- [x] `python -m compileall app tests alembic` — all clean
- [x] Docker build succeeds
- [x] Container starts, all 7 pollers cycle without errors (10 nodes in 3.0s)
- [x] Page navigation dramatically faster — no more 15-20s delays
- [x] All API endpoints return 200
- [x] Alembic `upgrade head` still works
- [ ] Run full test suite in Docker: `docker compose exec smp python -m unittest discover -s tests`

title: Update streams.md with XREADGROUP behavior details
body: Clarify the behavior of XREADGROUP in Active-Active streams, including message order guarantees.

title: Add stream consumer group tools
body:
## Summary

  This PR adds core Redis Streams consumer-group operations to the Redis MCP Server:

  - xgroup_create
  - xreadgroup
  - xack

  Redis already supports these commands, but mcp-redis only exposed xadd, xrange, and xdel. That meant MCP clients could write to streams and inspect them, but could not use Redis Streams for
  coordinated worker-style processing.

  ## Why

  The current stream tools support basic append/read/delete workflows, but they do not support the main Redis Streams pattern for distributed processing: consumer groups.

  Example:

  - With xadd + xrange, multiple workers can read the same stream entries, but Redis does not track ownership or completion.
  - With consumer groups, workers can read via xreadgroup, Redis tracks delivery to a specific consumer, and completed entries can be acknowledged with xack.

  This is useful for asynchronous and inference-adjacent workflows such as:

  - background job processing
  - embedding pipelines
  - document ingestion
  - multi-worker event handling
  - agent-triggered async task execution

  ## What Changed

  - Added xgroup_create to create a consumer group for a stream
  - Added xreadgroup to read stream entries through a consumer group
  - Added xack to acknowledge processed entries
  - Added unit tests for success cases, validation, and Redis error handling
  - Updated integration tests for MCP tool registration
  - Updated the README stream-tools description

  ## Validation

  Ran locally:
  uv run black src/tools/stream.py tests/tools/test_stream.py tests/test_integration.py
  uv run ruff check src/tools/stream.py tests/tools/test_stream.py tests/test_integration.py
  uv run pytest

  Result:
  - 321 passed

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Adds new MCP-exposed Redis Streams consumer-group operations (`XGROUP`/`XREADGROUP`/`XACK`), which may affect clients’ available tool surface and introduces new input-validation paths for blocking reads.
> 
> **Overview**
> Adds Redis Streams consumer-group support to the MCP server by introducing new tools: `xgroup_create`, `xgroup_destroy`, `xreadgroup`, and `xack` (including validation for `count` and `block_ms` and consistent Redis error-to-string handling).
> 
> Updates documentation to reflect the expanded Streams toolset, and extends unit/integration tests to assert tool registration, updated tool count, tool categorization, and new success/error/validation behaviors for the added stream consumer-group operations.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 7afb1921000f3b9f36b9d5a2d48b04cb27c8c46c. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

title: Bootstrap research & design project structure
body:
Repository was empty. Adds a minimal but extensible scaffold for capturing and iterating on new ideas across research, design, and prototyping phases.

## Structure
- `docs/research/01-overview.md` — methodology template (phases, key questions, research log)
- `docs/design/01-overview.md` — design principles, architecture diagram, component inventory, API contract placeholder, decision log
- `prototypes/hello_world/main.py` — thin three-layer (input → core → output) prototype demonstrating the pattern described in the design doc
- `prototypes/hello_world/test_main.py` — pytest coverage for each layer and end-to-end flow
- `.gitignore` — excludes Python caches, editor metadata, OS artefacts

## Architecture pattern

```python
def run(raw_input):
    data = input_layer(raw_input)   # validate & normalize
    result = core_logic(data)       # transform
    output_layer(result)            # deliver
    return result
```

All new prototypes should follow this layered convention and live under `prototypes/<name>/`.

title: Add Torch 2 and NumPy 2 compatibility fixes
body:
Summary:
This ports the fork forward enough to run training and validation on a modern Python environment used by esmart-ai-models.

Changes:
- add `weights_only=False` to `torch.load()` call sites for PyTorch 2.6+
- replace deprecated `torch.cuda.amp.autocast(...)` usage with `torch.amp.autocast('cuda', ...)`
- replace removed NumPy aliases (`np.int`) with built-in `int`
- replace AP integration code to support NumPy 2.x (`np.trapezoid` with fallback)
- replace Pillow 10-removed `ImageFont.getsize()` calls with `getbbox()`

Files touched:
- `train.py`
- `utils/aws/resume.py`
- `utils/datasets.py`
- `utils/general.py`
- `utils/loggers/__init__.py`
- `utils/loss.py`
- `utils/metrics.py`
- `utils/plots.py`

Validation:
- training startup fixed under Python 3.12
- AP computation validated with NumPy 2.4.3
- patch rebased onto `experiments`, which is the direct descendant branch of the submodule-pinned commit used in esmart-ai-models

body:
Bumps [pygments](https://github.com/pygments/pygments) from 2.19.2 to 2.20.0.
Release notes

Sourced from pygments's releases.

2.20.0

  • New lexers:

    • Rell (#2914)

  • Updated lexers:

    • archetype: Fix catastrophic backtracking in GUID and ID patterns (#3064)
    • ASN.1: Recognize minus sign and fix range operator (#3014, #3060)
    • C++: Add C++26 keywords (#2955), add integer literal suffixes (#2966)
    • ComponentPascal: Fix analyse_text (#3028, #3032)
    • Coq renamed to Rocq (#2883, #2908)
    • Cython: Various improvements (#2932, #2933)
    • Debian control: Improve architecture parsing (#3052)
    • Devicetree: Add support for overlay/fragments (#3021), add bytestring support (#3022), fix catastrophic backtracking (#3057)
    • Fennel: Various improvements (#2911)
    • Haskell: Handle escape sequences in character literals (#3069, #1795)
    • Java: Add module keywords (#2955)
    • Lean4: Add operators ]', ]?, ]! (#2946)
    • LESS: Support single-line comments (#3005)
    • LilyPond: Update to 2.25.29 (#2974)
